title: Reading the Print: Ladder Symbols and Rungs

questions.q: On a ladder diagram, which symbol is a Normally Open contact?
options: Two plain parallel lines  -| |-
Two lines with a diagonal slash  -|/|-
A circle  -( )-
A zig-zag line
questions.why: A Normally Open contact is drawn as two plain parallel lines; the slash through it would make it Normally Closed, and the circle is a coil.

questions.q: How do you read a single rung of a ladder diagram?
options: Right to left, coil first
Left to right, power passing through each contact to the coil
Top to bottom down the rail
In any order you like
questions.why: You read a rung left to right like a sentence: power must pass through every contact in the rung to reach and energize the coil at the right end.

questions.q: On a ladder rung, where does the coil (the circle) sit, and when is it energized?
options: On the left rail, energized always
At the far right, energized only when power passes through all contacts to its left
In the middle, energized by the neutral rail
On the right rail, energized by the fuse
questions.why: The coil sits at the far right end of the rung and energizes only when every contact to its left is closed so power can reach it.

questions.q: You see a rung: an NC contact, then a contact paralleled with another contact, all feeding a coil. Which familiar circuit is this?
options: A simple on/off light switch
The 3-wire Start/Stop seal-in latch
An on-delay timer only
A 3-phase motor power circuit
questions.why: That is the latch in ladder form: the NC Stop, then Start paralleled with the seal-in contact, all feeding the coil, exactly the 3-wire control circuit.

visual.caption: The rung as it appears on the print.

questions.q: You're handed a print with an unfamiliar rung: a normally-open contact labeled FU1-OK feeding a coil labeled M1-PERMIT, with no other symbols on the rung. Per the module's approach to reading a rung, what should you do before touching any wiring?
options: Ignore the print and go straight to the panel with your meter since the print could be outdated
Read it left to right like a sentence: power from the left rail must pass through the FU1-OK contact to energize M1-PERMIT, so trace what drives FU1-OK first
Assume the coil is always energized since it's on the right side of the rung
Skip the rung since a single-contact rung can't be a real fault path
questions.why: The module's core reading skill is left-to-right: identify what has to be true (FU1-OK closed) for the coil to energize, then trace backward to find what drives that contact. Going straight to the panel without reading the rung first throws away the print's biggest advantage -- turning a puzzle into a fast, directed check.

questions.q: You're probing a rung left to right at 3 AM. You read full 24VDC at the wire between the NC Stop symbol and the Start/seal-in parallel branch, but 0V on the wire between that branch and the coil. What do you conclude?
options: The gap is downstream of the parallel branch, so the fault is in that branch or its contacts, not the Stop button
The Stop button has failed and is blocking power
This is ghost voltage and can be ignored
The coil itself is open and needs replacing before checking anything else
questions.why: Full voltage going INTO the Start/seal-in branch but 0V coming OUT of it means the open is in that parallel section (bad Start contact and no seal-in path, or both legs open) -- not at Stop, which already passed voltage through. Jumping straight to 'replace the coil' skips the left-to-right rung trace that would have pinpointed the actual gap first.

visual.caption: The rung being probed — machine idle, buttons released.

questions.q: A tech reads 24V at both coil terminals with a standard high-impedance multimeter, but the relay won't pull in. Per the module's ghost voltage warning, what's the correct next move?
options: Replace the coil immediately since it's clearly getting full voltage but not pulling in
Switch the meter to LoZ mode (or clip a small load like a test light across the wire) and re-measure
Assume the print is wrong and rewire the rung from scratch
Increase the supply voltage to force the relay to pull in
questions.why: Reading 24V (to common) at BOTH coil terminals means the return path is open: power passes through the high-impedance coil winding, so both terminals float at 24V relative to common while the coil itself has 0V ACROSS it — no current, no pull-in. A LoZ meter (or a small test load) gives the measurement a path to common, and the return-side reading collapses to ~0V, confirming the open return. Replacing the coil (option 1) would fix nothing.

questions.q: You've been handed a rung to re-draw for a machine safety update. Where must the hardwired E-stop NC contact be placed relative to the Start/seal-in parallel branch, and why does it matter on the floor?
options: Anywhere on the rung, since ladder logic evaluates all contacts simultaneously regardless of position
Leftmost, before the parallel branch, so a welded seal-in contact can't create a path around it
Immediately to the right of the coil, so it's the last thing power sees before the coil
In parallel with the seal-in contact so it has a backup path
questions.why: The E-stop must sit leftmost so it's in series with every possible path to the coil, including a welded/sneaking seal-in branch; put it to the right of that branch (or in parallel with it) and power can sneak around the E-stop entirely even with the button pressed. Left-to-right position on a rung is a safety argument, not just drawing style.

questions.q: A 24VDC relay coil has a rated pull-in of 90% of rated voltage. If three pitted contacts in series on the feed to that coil are each dropping 1.5V under load, what happens and why?
options: Nothing -- coil voltage only matters at the coil terminals, not upstream contact drops
The coil sees roughly 19.5V, which is still above the ~21.6V pull-in threshold so it operates normally
The coil sees roughly 19.5V, below the ~21.6V (90% of 24V) pull-in threshold, so it may fail to pull in or chatter
The contacts will blow a fuse before the coil is affected
questions.why: 3 contacts x 1.5V = 4.5V lost in series before the coil, leaving about 19.5V at the coil -- below the 21.6VDC (90% of 24V) pull-in minimum the module calls out, so the relay can fail to pull in or chatter even though the supply at the left rail reads a healthy 24V. Voltage drop stacks across every series contact, it doesn't just matter at the coil terminals.

questions.q: A technician re-drawing a machine's E-stop rung moves the hardwired E-stop contact to the right of the Start/seal-in parallel branch instead of leftmost, reasoning 'it still opens the circuit either way.' What is the real danger if this rung goes into service as drawn?
options: None -- as long as the E-stop contact is somewhere on the rung, pressing it will always de-energize the coil
The rung will simply run slower, causing a delay before the coil drops out
If the seal-in contact welds or sneaks power around that branch, pressing E-stop no longer breaks the only path to the coil, so the machine keeps running with E-stop pressed
The coil will draw excess current and burn out, but the E-stop function itself is unaffected
questions.why: Position on the rung matters: with E-stop to the right of the parallel branch, a welded or sneaking seal-in contact provides an alternate path to the coil that bypasses the E-stop contact entirely -- pressing E-stop won't stop the machine, which is exactly the hazard the module warns about. 'It opens the circuit either way' is only true if every other contact is healthy, and E-stop rungs must be designed for the failure case, not the healthy case.

questions.q: In the print shown, what must be true for coil CR1 to energize?
options: PB1 and PB2 must both be open
Only PB2 matters — PB1 is a spare contact on the rung
PB1 must stay closed AND at least one of PB2 or the CR1 contact must close — series first, then the parallel pair
The CR1 contact must open to let power through to the coil
questions.why: Read the rung left to right: power crosses FU1, then the series contact PB1, then the parallel branch — PB2 OR the CR1 contact — before it can reach the coil. Series means AND, parallel means OR.

visual.caption: The rung as it appears on the print — read it left to right.
